title: Hypercalcemia of Malignancy
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Oncology Fellows, Endocrinology, Critical Care
objectives: Understand mechanisms of hypercalcemia of malignancy (PTHrP, osteolytic, calcitriol)
Initiate appropriate treatment (hydration, calcitonin, bisphosphonates, denosumab)
Recognize poor prognosis associated with HCM

guidelineReferences.name: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Treatment of Hypercalcemia of Malignancy
guidelineReferences.citation: Goldner W, et al. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2023;108(3):507-528
keyPoints: Denosumab or IV bisphosphonate recommended for all HCM
Add calcitonin for severe HCM (Ca >14 mg/dL)

vignette.text:
A 68-year-old woman with metastatic squamous cell lung cancer presents with confusion, severe constipation, polyuria, and profound weakness over 2 weeks. She has known bone metastases and was recently started on immunotherapy. She appears dehydrated and lethargic.

Past Medical History: Squamous cell lung cancer with bone metastases, Type 2 diabetes, CKD stage 3

Medications: Pembrolizumab, metformin, lisinopril

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.general: Cachectic, lethargic, oriented to person only
physicalExam.pulmonary: Decreased breath sounds at right base
physicalExam.neuro: Lethargic, slow responses, no focal deficits

questions.stem: This patient has elevated PTHrP and suppressed PTH.
questions.question: What are the mechanisms of hypercalcemia of malignancy?

options.text: HUMORAL (PTHrP-mediated, 80%): Tumor secretes PTHrP → bone resorption, renal calcium retention; squamous cell cancers, renal, breast; LOCAL OSTEOLYTIC (20%): Direct bone metastases → local cytokine release → bone destruction; breast, myeloma, lung; CALCITRIOL-MEDIATED (<1%): Lymphoma produces 1,25-vitamin D; PTH-MEDIATED: Parathyroid carcinoma (rare)
HCM is always due to bone metastases

questions.explanation: Mechanisms of HCM: (1) HUMORAL HYPERCALCEMIA (PTHrP) - 80%: Tumor produces PTHrP (structurally similar to PTH) → increased bone resorption, increased renal calcium reabsorption, decreased phosphorus reabsorption. Common: Squamous cell carcinomas (lung, head/neck), renal cell, breast, bladder; (2) LOCAL OSTEOLYTIC - 20%: Direct bone destruction by metastases releasing cytokines (IL-6, TNF, RANKL). Common: Breast cancer, multiple myeloma, lung; (3) CALCITRIOL-MEDIATED - <1%: Tumor produces 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D. Common: Hodgkin/non-Hodgkin lymphoma, ovarian dysgerminoma; (4) PTH-MEDIATED: Parathyroid carcinoma or ectopic PTH (extremely rare). This patient: PTHrP-mediated (humoral) - suppressed PTH, elevated PTHrP, squamous cell cancer.

whyChain.answer: Elevated calcium from any cause suppresses parathyroid glands via calcium-sensing receptor. In HCM, the hypercalcemia is caused by PTHrP (not PTH), so PTH is appropriately suppressed. Suppressed PTH with elevated calcium rules out primary hyperparathyroidism and confirms malignancy-related or other non-PTH cause.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has calcium 16.6 mg/dL (corrected) with confusion and lethargy.
questions.question: How is hypercalcemia severity classified and what are the symptoms?

options.text: MILD: Ca 10.5-12 mg/dL (often asymptomatic); MODERATE: Ca 12-14 mg/dL (fatigue, constipation, polyuria); SEVERE: Ca >14 mg/dL (confusion, stupor, coma, cardiac arrhythmias - ONCOLOGIC EMERGENCY); Mnemonic 'Stones, Bones, Groans, Moans, Psychiatric Overtones' - but HCM typically presents acutely with severe symptoms

questions.explanation: Hypercalcemia severity: MILD (10.5-12 mg/dL): Often asymptomatic or vague fatigue; MODERATE (12-14 mg/dL): Fatigue, constipation, nausea, polyuria, polydipsia; SEVERE (>14 mg/dL): Altered mental status (confusion → stupor → coma), cardiac arrhythmias, severe dehydration, AKI - ONCOLOGIC EMERGENCY. Symptoms by system: GI (constipation, nausea, anorexia, pancreatitis), Renal (polyuria, nephrolithiasis, AKI), Neuro (confusion, weakness, lethargy, coma), Cardiac (shortened QT, arrhythmias, bradycardia). This patient: SEVERE HCM (16.6) with encephalopathy, AKI, dehydration.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to confirm the etiology of hypercalcemia.
questions.question: What is the diagnostic workup for hypercalcemia of malignancy?

options.text: Imaging is the only test needed

Corrected calcium (for albumin) or ionized calcium; PTH (suppressed in HCM vs elevated in hyperparathyroidism); PTHrP (elevated in humoral HCM); 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (elevated in lymphoma); Phosphorus (low in PTHrP-mediated); Creatinine (assess AKI); 25-hydroxyvitamin D (rule out toxicity)
PTH is always elevated in cancer

questions.explanation: HCM diagnostic workup: (1) CALCIUM: Correct for albumin (add 0.8 mg/dL per 1 g/dL albumin <4) or check ionized calcium; (2) PTH: Key discriminator - SUPPRESSED in HCM (<20), ELEVATED in primary hyperparathyroidism; (3) PTHrP: Elevated in humoral HCM (80% of cases); (4) 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D: Elevated in lymphoma-associated HCM; (5) Phosphorus: Low/normal in PTHrP (PTHrP promotes phosphaturia), elevated in osteolytic; (6) Creatinine: Assess AKI (common); (7) 25-hydroxyvitamin D: Rule out vitamin D toxicity. This patient: Corrected Ca 16.6, suppressed PTH, elevated PTHrP → humoral HCM from squamous cell cancer.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to initiate urgent treatment for this severely hypercalcemic patient.
questions.question: What is the initial treatment for severe hypercalcemia of malignancy?

options.text: Step 1: AGGRESSIVE IV HYDRATION with normal saline (200-300 mL/hr initially, adjust for volume status and cardiac function) - dilutes calcium, promotes calciuresis; Step 2: CALCITONIN 4 IU/kg SC/IM q12h for severe HCM (Ca >14) - rapid onset (4-6h) but tachyphylaxis by 48-72h; Step 3: Antiresorptive therapy (bisphosphonate or denosumab); Avoid loop diuretics unless volume overloaded

questions.explanation: Initial HCM treatment: (1) IV HYDRATION: Normal saline 200-500 mL/hr initially (adjust for cardiac/renal status), goal 2-4 L in first 24 hours. Mechanism: Volume expansion dilutes calcium, increases GFR, promotes calciuresis; (2) CALCITONIN (for severe HCM >14 mg/dL): 4 IU/kg SC or IM q12h. Rapid onset (4-6 hours), but TACHYPHYLAXIS develops by 48-72 hours - use as bridge to bisphosphonates/denosumab; (3) Antiresorptive therapy: Start simultaneously with hydration/calcitonin. LOOP DIURETICS: Only if volume overloaded - do NOT give routinely as they worsen dehydration.

whyChain.answer: Historically, loop diuretics were used to promote calciuresis. However, they also cause volume depletion (worsening hypercalcemia), hypokalemia, and hypomagnesemia. Current guidelines: Use ONLY if patient is volume overloaded after adequate hydration, not as routine treatment.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to choose between bisphosphonates and denosumab.
questions.question: What antiresorptive therapy is recommended for HCM?

options.text: Endocrine Society recommends DENOSUMAB over bisphosphonates; ZOLEDRONIC ACID 4mg IV over 15 min (most potent bisphosphonate) - onset 2-4 days, duration 2-4 weeks, renally cleared; DENOSUMAB 120mg SC - not renally cleared (preferred in renal impairment), onset 3-4 days, can repeat weekly for 4 weeks initially; Wait ≥7 days before repeating bisphosphonate

questions.explanation: Antiresorptive therapy (2022 Endocrine Society Guidelines): DENOSUMAB PREFERRED over bisphosphonates (conditional recommendation). BISPHOSPHONATES: Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV over 15 min (most potent, most commonly used), Pamidronate 60-90 mg IV over 2-4 hours. Onset: 2-4 days, Peak: 4-7 days, Duration: 2-4 weeks. Renally cleared - reduce dose or avoid in severe CKD. Don't repeat <7 days. DENOSUMAB: 120 mg SC. Onset: 3-4 days. NOT renally cleared - preferred in renal impairment. Can give weekly x4 initially, then monthly. May cause profound hypocalcemia. This patient: Consider denosumab given her CKD (Cr 2.1).

whyChain.answer: Denosumab advantages: (1) Not renally cleared - safe in renal impairment; (2) Effective in bisphosphonate-refractory HCM (64-70% response); (3) Can be dosed more frequently initially. Disadvantages: Risk of severe hypocalcemia (especially in renal impairment), longer duration of effect makes hypocalcemia harder to manage.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A different patient has hypercalcemia from lymphoma.
questions.question: How is calcitriol-mediated hypercalcemia (lymphoma) treated?

options.text: GLUCOCORTICOIDS first-line (prednisone 40-60 mg/day or equivalent) - suppress 1-alpha-hydroxylase in tumor; If severe or refractory despite steroids, ADD denosumab or bisphosphonate; Onset of steroid effect: 2-5 days; Also applies to granulomatous diseases (sarcoidosis) causing hypercalcemia

questions.explanation: Calcitriol-mediated hypercalcemia (lymphoma, granulomatous disease): GLUCOCORTICOIDS are first-line. Mechanism: Suppress extra-renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase activity that converts 25-OH vitamin D to active 1,25-OH vitamin D. Dosing: Prednisone 40-60 mg/day or hydrocortisone 200-300 mg/day IV. Onset: 2-5 days. If severe or persistent despite steroids: Add bisphosphonate or denosumab. Also applies to: Sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, other granulomatous diseases, some ovarian tumors.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.

C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is GLUCOCORTICOIDS first-line (prednisone 40-60 mg/day or equivalent) - suppress 1-alpha-hydroxylase in tumor; If severe or refractory despite steroids, ADD denosumab or bisphosphonate; Onset of steroid effect: 2-5 days; Also applies to granulomatous diseases (sarcoidosis) causing hypercalcemia because Calcitriol-mediated hypercalcemia (lymphoma, granulomatous disease): GLUCOCORTICOIDS are first-line. Mechanism: Suppress

questions.stem: Another patient has refractory hypercalcemia despite zoledronic acid.
questions.question: How is refractory/recurrent HCM managed?

options.text: No options exist for refractory HCM

DENOSUMAB is recommended for bisphosphonate-refractory HCM (64-70% achieve response); May also consider: Repeat bisphosphonate (wait ≥7 days), Hemodialysis with low-calcium dialysate for severe/life-threatening HCM with AKI, Gallium nitrate (if available), Cinacalcet (for parathyroid carcinoma)

questions.explanation: Refractory/recurrent HCM management: (1) DENOSUMAB: Recommended for bisphosphonate-refractory HCM - 64-70% response rate in patients who failed bisphosphonates; (2) Repeat bisphosphonate: Wait at least 7 days, may try different agent; (3) HEMODIALYSIS: For severe, life-threatening HCM with renal failure - use low-calcium dialysate; (4) Gallium nitrate: Effective but limited availability, requires continuous infusion; (5) Cinacalcet: For parathyroid carcinoma specifically. Ultimate treatment: Control of underlying malignancy is essential for durable HCM control.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You are discussing prognosis with the patient's family.
questions.question: What is the prognosis of hypercalcemia of malignancy?

options.text: HCM indicates POOR PROGNOSIS - median survival often weeks to months; Reflects advanced, often refractory cancer; Higher calcium levels and PTHrP correlate with worse survival; Treatment provides symptom relief and may enable further cancer therapy; Addressing underlying malignancy is essential for HCM control
Prognosis is unrelated to cancer status

questions.explanation: HCM prognosis: POOR - median survival often weeks to 3-4 months. HCM typically indicates advanced malignancy with high tumor burden. Higher calcium levels and higher PTHrP correlate with worse outcomes. Importance of treatment: (1) Symptom relief - improves quality of life significantly; (2) Enables further cancer therapy - can't give chemotherapy to confused, dehydrated patient; (3) Prevents complications (arrhythmias, renal failure, coma). Long-term HCM control depends on treating underlying malignancy. Goals of care discussion often appropriate.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Returning to the original patient with severe PTHrP-mediated HCM.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Immediate: ICU admission, cardiac monitoring (QT), IV NS 200-300 mL/hr (adjust for cardiac status), Calcitonin 4 IU/kg SC q12h (bridge therapy x48-72h), Denosumab 120mg SC (preferred given CKD); Monitoring: Calcium, creatinine q6-12h, watch for hypocalcemia after denosumab; Supportive: Hold nephrotoxins (lisinopril), treat underlying cancer when stable; Prognosis discussion with family

questions.explanation: Complete management for this patient: IMMEDIATE: (1) ICU/step-down admission, continuous cardiac monitoring (arrhythmia risk); (2) Aggressive IV NS hydration (200-300 mL/hr, adjust for cardiac status); (3) CALCITONIN 4 IU/kg SC q12h - bridge therapy while waiting for antiresorptive effect (limit to 48-72h due to tachyphylaxis); (4) DENOSUMAB 120mg SC - preferred over bisphosphonate given her CKD (Cr 2.1); (5) Hold nephrotoxins (lisinopril, metformin). MONITORING: Calcium q6-12h, renal function, phosphorus. Watch for hypocalcemia after denosumab (especially with renal impairment). AFTER STABILIZATION: Resume/modify cancer therapy; Goals of care discussion given poor prognosis associated with HCM; May need repeated denosumab every 4 weeks.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected hypercalcemia of malignancy?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in hypercalcemia of malignancy. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in hypercalcemia of malignancy. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in hypercalcemia of malignancy. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of hypercalcemia of malignancy. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

mcqs.stem: When managing hypercalcemia of malignancy, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

mcqs.stem: A 35-year-old presents with petechiae, confusion, renal insufficiency, fever, and platelets of 18,000 with schistocytes on smear. What is the most critical immediate intervention?

options.explanation: TTP is distinguished from DIC by NORMAL coagulation studies (PT, PTT, fibrinogen). DIC shows prolonged PT/PTT and low fibrinogen. Treatment differs fundamentally — TTP requires plasmapheresis, not anticoagulation.

options.text: Obtain ADAMTS13 activity level and treat based on results
options.explanation: ADAMTS13 testing takes 2-3 days. With suspected TTP, mortality approaches 90% without treatment. Plasma exchange must begin on clinical suspicion while awaiting confirmatory testing.

options.text: Platelet transfusion to prevent intracranial hemorrhage
options.explanation: Platelet transfusion in TTP is CONTRAINDICATED (relative) as it can fuel thrombotic microangiopathy and worsen organ damage. Platelets are consumed in microvascular thrombi, not bleeding.

options.text: Emergent plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) — do not wait for ADAMTS13 results
options.explanation: This pentad (thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia with schistocytes, neurologic symptoms, renal dysfunction, fever) is classic TTP. Per ASH guidelines, plasma exchange must be initiated IMMEDIATELY — mortality without treatment exceeds 90%. Do NOT wait for ADAMTS13 activity levels; start treatment on clinical suspicion.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Emergent plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) — do not wait for ADAMTS13 results.
